scenes.title: Semantic Graph Demo
scenes.description: Demonstrates the semantic graph dock view.
scenes.markdown:
## Semantic Graph Demo

Switch to the **Graph** tab in the left dock to view interactive Mermaid diagrams for each proof step. Each equation appears in both its canonical form and a physically-equivalent alternate form, so you can see how the semantic graph reshapes as the algebra is rewritten.

steps.title: Higher powers and a quotient (^3, ^4, ^n, ^-3, ^-4, ^-n, a/b)
Annotation: two assumptions
steps.description: $P V = n R T \quad (T \text{ constant}) \quad (n \text{ fixed})$

steps.title: Annotation: paragraph-length
steps.description: $\Delta S \geq 0 \quad (\text{holds for isolated systems undergoing irreversible processes where no heat exchange with surroundings occurs})$

steps.title: Domain constraint: α ∈ ℂ
steps.description: α ∈ ℂ — styled symbol correctly parsed

proof.title: Physics Equations: Classical → Relativistic → Quantum
proof.goal: Inspect semantic graphs for canonical physics equations, each shown in two equivalent forms

steps.label: Newton's Second Law
steps.justification: The canonical form of Newton's Second Law: net force equals inertial mass times acceleration. This is the workhorse of classical mechanics — a statement that the same push produces less acceleration on a heavier object, making mass the measure of inertia.

nodes.description: Equality relating the resulting force on the left to the product of mass and acceleration on the right — the root of the semantic graph.

nodes.label: force
nodes.description: Net force: the vector sum of all forces acting on the object. Measured in newtons (N = kg·m/s²). In this law, force is the *cause* — the external push or pull that changes the object's motion.
Multiplication combining the scalar inertia (mass) with the vector quantity (acceleration). The result is a vector pointing in the same direction as the acceleration.

nodes.label: mass
nodes.description: Inertial mass: the object's resistance to changes in its motion. A larger mass means a smaller acceleration for the same applied force. Treated as a positive, time-independent scalar parameter.

nodes.label: acceleration
nodes.description: Acceleration: the time rate of change of velocity, dv/dt. A vector quantity — both the magnitude (how quickly speed changes) and direction (which way velocity is changing) matter. Responds linearly to applied force.

steps.label: Newton's Second Law (momentum form)
steps.justification: The more general form of Newton's Second Law: force equals the time rate of change of momentum. This reduces to F = m·a when mass is constant, but correctly handles variable-mass systems (rockets, raindrops) and serves as the natural bridge to relativistic mechanics.

nodes.description: Equates force to the time derivative of momentum. In this form, force is exactly the quantity that changes momentum — the cleaner statement Newton actually wrote.

nodes.label: force
nodes.description: Net force on the system. Identical conceptually to the F in F = m·a, but here it is defined as *whatever it takes* to change the system's momentum — a definition that survives into relativistic mechanics.
Time derivative of momentum, dp/dt. Measures how rapidly the system's momentum changes. Equal to m·a for constant-mass systems; non-trivially different when dm/dt ≠ 0.

nodes.label: momentum
nodes.description: Linear momentum, p = m·v. The primary conserved quantity of translation symmetry — total p of an isolated system is constant (Noether's theorem). Vector; points in the direction of motion.

nodes.label: time
nodes.description: Time: the independent variable with respect to which momentum evolves. Treated as the universal Newtonian parameter (same for all observers) — relativity modifies this later.

steps.label: Mass-energy equivalence
steps.justification: Einstein's famous result: the rest energy of a body equals its rest mass multiplied by the speed of light squared. This is the low-momentum limit of the full relativistic energy relation, and it exposes mass as a form of frozen energy.

nodes.description: Equality declaring that rest energy and the quantity m·c² are the same physical thing — not merely proportional, but literally equal.

nodes.label: energy
nodes.description: Rest energy: the intrinsic energy an object carries by virtue of its mass alone (i.e. at zero momentum in its own rest frame). Equivalent to about 9 × 10¹⁶ J per kilogram — enormous, because c² is enormous.
Multiplies rest mass by c². The factor c² is the conversion rate between the two ways nature accounts for inertia — mass units and energy units.

nodes.label: mass
nodes.description: Rest mass (invariant mass): the mass measured in the object's own rest frame. Unlike the obsolete 'relativistic mass', rest mass is a Lorentz invariant — every observer agrees on its value.

Squares the speed of light. The square arises from relativity's spacetime metric, which mixes time with space using an interval of the form (c·t)² − x². Whenever energy appears, c² is its natural companion.

nodes.label: speed of light
nodes.description: Speed of light in vacuum: a universal constant, exactly 299,792,458 m/s by definition. It is the maximum signal speed, the conversion factor between space and time coordinates, and the scale on which mass-energy equivalence operates.

steps.label: Relativistic energy-momentum relation
steps.justification: The full Lorentz-invariant energy relation. It reduces to E = mc² at rest (p = 0) and to E = pc for massless particles (m = 0, such as photons). Forms a Pythagorean triangle whose legs are momentum-energy and rest-energy; the hypotenuse is total energy.

nodes.description: Relates the square of total energy to the sum of the squared momentum-energy and squared rest-energy terms. Manifestly Lorentz invariant: both sides are the same four-vector invariant.
Squares the total energy. Appears quadratically because the invariant is the norm of the four-momentum, which mixes E and p through the Minkowski metric.

nodes.label: energy
nodes.description: Total relativistic energy: includes both the rest energy (mc²) and the kinetic contribution from motion. For a particle at rest this collapses to E = mc²; for a photon (m = 0) it collapses to E = pc.
Pythagorean sum of the momentum-energy contribution (pc)² and the rest-energy contribution (mc²)². Geometrically, E is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs pc and mc².
Squares the momentum-energy product pc. This is the purely-kinetic contribution to total energy and the only term that survives for massless particles.
Product of momentum and the speed of light — pc has units of energy and represents the 'motion portion' of relativistic energy.

nodes.label: momentum
nodes.description: Relativistic momentum: p = γ·m·v where γ is the Lorentz factor. For massless particles (photons) momentum is defined purely by p = E/c — mass is not required to carry momentum.

nodes.label: speed of light
nodes.description: Universal constant that couples space and time. Exactly 299,792,458 m/s by definition of the metre. Shared between the pc and mc² terms because it's the same fundamental conversion factor in both.
Squares the rest energy mc². This term contributes the invariant mass-energy floor — the energy a particle has even at rest.
Forms mc², the rest energy, before it is squared. The same object that appears alone in the low-momentum Einstein equation E = mc².
Inner c² in the rest-energy leg. Squaring c arises from the Minkowski metric's time component, the same way it does in E = mc².

nodes.label: mass
nodes.description: Rest (invariant) mass. Same quantity as in E = mc² — a Lorentz scalar that every observer agrees on, unrelated to the particle's velocity.

steps.label: Uniform acceleration (velocity form)
steps.justification: First kinematic equation for uniformly accelerated motion. Integrating a constant acceleration once with respect to time yields this linear-in-t velocity law.

nodes.description: States that the instantaneous velocity equals the initial velocity plus the velocity gained through constant acceleration over time t.

nodes.label: velocity
nodes.description: Final velocity at time t. A vector: direction matters. For straight-line motion with constant a it evolves linearly in time; for curved motion the equation applies component-wise.
Sums the initial velocity with the velocity change acquired through acceleration. The two addends have the same physical units (velocity), which is what makes the sum meaningful.

nodes.label: initial velocity
nodes.description: Initial velocity at t = 0: the boundary condition fixing where the velocity trajectory begins. Vector quantity, same units as v.
Multiplies the (constant) acceleration by elapsed time to produce the velocity accumulated since t = 0.

nodes.label: acceleration
nodes.description: Constant acceleration over the motion. The uniform-a assumption is what makes this equation linear; general motion requires integrating a(t).

nodes.label: time
nodes.description: Elapsed time since the initial moment t = 0. The independent variable — plotting v versus t yields a straight line with slope a and intercept v₀.

steps.label: Uniform acceleration (position form)
steps.math: x = x_0 + v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2
steps.justification: Second kinematic equation — integrating v(t) = v₀ + a·t with respect to time yields a quadratic-in-t position law. The ½ factor emerges from the integral ∫t dt = ½t².

nodes.description: Sets position equal to a three-term sum: starting position, linear drift from initial velocity, and quadratic drift from constant acceleration.

nodes.label: position
nodes.description: Position at time t. A vector in general; its time evolution is quadratic under constant acceleration, producing the familiar parabolic trajectories of projectiles.
Three-way sum: initial position + linear displacement + quadratic displacement. Each term must carry units of length for the sum to be well-formed.

nodes.label: initial position
nodes.description: Initial position at t = 0 — the fixed starting point of the trajectory. Boundary condition: changes the intercept but not the shape of the x(t) curve.
Linear drift term v₀·t: the distance covered by inertial motion at the initial velocity, if there were no acceleration.

nodes.label: initial velocity
nodes.description: Initial velocity at t = 0. Unlike the velocity form, here it multiplies t directly (no acceleration coupling) — it accounts for whatever motion was already underway when the clock started.
Quadratic drift term ½·a·t² — the additional displacement produced by constant acceleration. The ½ is the integral of t, not a physical coefficient.

nodes.label: one half
nodes.description: The rational constant ½, produced by integrating t with respect to t. Dimensionless.

nodes.label: acceleration
nodes.description: Constant acceleration vector. Assumed time-independent in this kinematic family of equations — relaxing this assumption breaks the simple quadratic form.
Squares time. Its presence is what makes free-fall trajectories parabolas — the displacement-vs-time curve under constant a.

nodes.label: time
nodes.description: Elapsed time since t = 0. Same independent variable as in the velocity form; appears linearly (in the v₀t term) and quadratically (in the ½at² term).

steps.label: Schrödinger equation (compact Hamiltonian form)
steps.justification: The time-dependent Schrödinger equation in abstract form: the quantum state ψ evolves in time at the rate set by the Hamiltonian operator Ĥ. The complex factor iℏ on the left is what makes quantum evolution unitary (probability-preserving) rather than diffusive.

nodes.description: Governing equation of non-relativistic quantum mechanics. Determines how the state vector ψ evolves given the system's Hamiltonian (total-energy operator).

Combines i, ℏ, and the partial time derivative of ψ into the left-hand side. The ordering of these scalar multiplications is commutative, but the imaginary i is what ensures wave-like (rather than dissipative) dynamics.

nodes.label: imaginary unit
nodes.description: The imaginary unit, i = √(−1). Pulls the time derivative into the complex plane — without it, ∂/∂t would describe diffusion (real-valued decay); with it, we get oscillatory wavefunctions and interference.

nodes.label: reduced Planck constant
nodes.description: ℏ = h/(2π) ≈ 1.054 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s. Sets the scale of quantum effects — actions much larger than ℏ behave classically; actions of order ℏ require quantum mechanics.
Partial time derivative of the wavefunction. Measures how rapidly ψ changes in time at a fixed spatial point — the object the Schrödinger equation prescribes.

nodes.label: wavefunction
nodes.description: Quantum state (wavefunction). A complex-valued function of position and time whose squared modulus |ψ|² gives the probability density. Normalized such that ∫|ψ|² dV = 1.

nodes.label: Hamiltonian
nodes.description: Hamiltonian operator Ĥ — the total-energy operator of the system. Typically Ĥ = T̂ + V̂ (kinetic plus potential). Hermitian (real eigenvalues = allowed energies), and it generates time translation via the Schrödinger equation. When ψ feeds in, the output is Ĥψ — the *action* of the operator on the state, not a product.

steps.label: Schrödinger equation (expanded)
steps.justification: The Schrödinger equation with the Hamiltonian spelled out as kinetic + potential. The kinetic term -ℏ²/(2m)·∇² arises from quantizing p²/(2m) via p̂ = -iℏ∇. This is the PDE that is actually solved in textbooks — hydrogen atom, particle in a box, harmonic oscillator.

nodes.description: Equates the time evolution of ψ (left) to the sum of kinetic and potential contributions (right) — the Hamiltonian in position representation.
Forms i·ℏ·∂ψ/∂t — same left-hand side as in the compact form.

nodes.label: imaginary unit
nodes.description: The imaginary unit i = √(−1). Essential: without i, ∂/∂t would describe diffusion; with i, probability is conserved and we get unitary evolution.

nodes.label: reduced Planck constant
nodes.description: ℏ ≈ 1.054 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s. Sets the scale where quantum effects matter. Appears squared in the kinetic term — a direct consequence of quantizing p = -iℏ∇ and then forming p²/(2m).
Partial time derivative of ψ: how the wavefunction changes instantaneously at each point in space.

nodes.label: wavefunction
nodes.description: Complex-valued wavefunction ψ(r, t). |ψ|² gives probability density; phase matters too (interference). Shared across the time derivative, the Laplacian, and the potential multiplication.
Hamiltonian in position representation: kinetic energy operator applied to ψ plus potential energy times ψ.
Assembles the kinetic term: (−1) · [ℏ²/(2m)] · ∇²ψ. The minus sign is absorbed via a literal −1 multiplier.

nodes.label: negative one
nodes.description: The numerical constant −1, standing in for the explicit minus sign on the kinetic term.
Divides ℏ² by 2m to produce the kinetic coefficient. Larger mass → smaller coefficient → weaker quantum-kinetic spreading.
Squares ℏ. The ℏ² factor arises because the kinetic operator is p̂²/(2m) and p̂ carries one factor of ℏ.
Forms 2m — the denominator of the kinetic coefficient, same factor that appears in the classical expression p²/(2m).

nodes.label: two
nodes.description: The numerical constant 2, from the classical kinetic-energy formula p²/(2m).

nodes.label: mass
nodes.description: Mass of the quantum particle. Determines how strongly the kinetic operator smooths / spreads the wavefunction — lighter particles exhibit more pronounced quantum behavior.

nodes.label: Laplacian
nodes.description: Laplacian operator ∇² applied to ψ: ∇²ψ = ∂²ψ/∂x² + ∂²ψ/∂y² + ∂²ψ/∂z². Measures the local curvature of the wavefunction; wavefunctions with sharper spatial variations carry more kinetic energy. Like Ĥ, ∇² is an operator that acts on ψ — not a multiplication.
Pointwise product V(r)·ψ(r,t). The potential acts as a multiplicative operator in the position representation.

nodes.label: potential energy
nodes.description: Potential energy function V(r) — an external scalar field (e.g. electrostatic for hydrogen, harmonic for an oscillator, infinite walls for a box). Shapes the Hamiltonian's spectrum and the geometry of stationary states.

steps.label: Partial derivatives — 2D transport equation
steps.justification: The 2D advection (transport) equation: density ρ is carried along by a velocity field (u, v) without diffusion. Each partial derivative operator differentiates with respect to a different independent variable — t, x, or y — so the D3 renderer shows d·/dt, d·/dx, and d·/dy on distinct operator nodes.

nodes.description: Equates the sum of partial derivatives to zero — stating that ρ is conserved along fluid trajectories.
Three-term sum: the local time rate of change plus the two advective flux contributions.
Partial time derivative of density: how ρ changes at a fixed spatial point.
Advective flux in x: the horizontal velocity u times the spatial gradient ∂ρ/∂x.

nodes.label: x-velocity
nodes.description: Horizontal component of the velocity field. Determines how fast ρ is carried in the x direction.
Partial x-derivative of density: the spatial gradient of ρ in the horizontal direction.
Advective flux in y: the vertical velocity v times the spatial gradient ∂ρ/∂y.

nodes.label: y-velocity
nodes.description: Vertical component of the velocity field. Determines how fast ρ is carried in the y direction.
Partial y-derivative of density: the spatial gradient of ρ in the vertical direction.

nodes.label: density
nodes.description: Scalar density field ρ(x, y, t). The quantity being transported by the velocity field without sources or sinks.
Zero on the right-hand side — no sources or sinks.

nodes.label: time
nodes.description: Independent variable: time coordinate.

nodes.label: x-position
nodes.description: Independent variable: horizontal spatial coordinate.

nodes.label: y-position
nodes.description: Independent variable: vertical spatial coordinate.

steps.label: Higher powers and a quotient — ^3, ^4, ^n, ^-3, ^-4, ^-n, a/b
steps.justification: Positive integer exponents read as direct (red) at increasing widths; negative integer exponents read as inverse (blue) at the same widths. The symbolic −n is also tagged inverse with default weight 1 — the renderer can read sign from the leading minus even without a magnitude. Only the positive symbolic n stays neutral. The quotient a/b shows the multiply-with-denominator pattern: the numerator's edge from a paints direct (factor); the edge from the inverse-power (b^-1) paints inverse — the multiply combiner no longer overrides the renderer's power-source inference.

nodes.description: Equates y to a seven-term sum: six monomials in x plus a quotient a/b.

nodes.label: y
nodes.description: Dependent variable — its value is determined by x, a, and b once the seven terms are summed.

nodes.label: x
nodes.description: Shared base for the six power monomials. Reused across the diagram so the contrast between the six outgoing power-edges is the only visual difference.

nodes.label: a
nodes.description: Numerator of the quotient term. Its edge into the multiply combiner is tagged direct (every multiplicative factor is linearly proportional to the product).

nodes.label: b
nodes.description: Denominator of the quotient term. Its edge into the inverse-power node is plain — the proportionality semantics live on the *outgoing* edge from the power.
Sums the seven terms (six monomials + the a/b quotient). Addition edges are structural — they don't carry proportionality semantics.
Cubes x. The renderer reads this exponent and tags the *outgoing* edge as direct with weight 3 (clamped to the theme's max stroke width).
Raises x to the fourth power. Outgoing edge tagged direct, weight 4 — saturates the clamp on themes with a base direct-stroke ≥ 2.
Raises x to the symbolic power n. The inference rule fires only when sign can be determined; positive symbolic exponents have no usable sign or magnitude, so this node's outgoing edge stays neutral.
Raises x to the −3 power (i.e. 1/x³). The negative literal exponent tags the *outgoing* edge as inverse with weight 3, painted in the inverse stroke from the active theme.
Raises x to the −4 power (i.e. 1/x⁴). Outgoing edge tagged inverse, weight 4.
Raises x to the symbolic −n power. The leading minus tells the renderer the relationship is inverse; magnitude is unknown, so it defaults to weight 1 (thin blue stroke).

Reciprocal of b — i.e. 1/b. SymPy represents division `a/b` as `Mul(a, Pow(b, -1))`, so the quotient enters the diagram via this inverse-power node. Its outgoing edge into the multiply combiner is tagged inverse, weight 1.

Combines the numerator a with the inverse-power b^{-1} to form the quotient a·(1/b). Factor edges from regular operands are tagged direct; the edge from an inverse-power child is left plain so the renderer's power-source inference paints it inverse instead of overriding it.

steps.label: Disconnected statements — comma-separated clauses (issue #144)
steps.justification: Top-level commas are detected as statement separators (not logical operators, not tuple separators). Each clause is parsed into its own independent subtree with no parent relation node joining them. With no shared variables between the two clauses, the resulting graph has two truly disconnected components — the renderer lays them out side-by-side. Shared variables across clauses (for example γ appearing in both dh/dt = -V sin γ and γ = const) would still dedup organically through the variable node.

nodes.description: First statement's equality — the root of clause 0's subtree. Parsed independently from clause 1 after the top-level comma was detected as a statement separator.

nodes.label: a
nodes.description: Free symbol declared in the first statement. No semantic connection to clause 1's b.
Numeric literal on the RHS of clause 0.
Second statement's equality — the root of clause 1's subtree. Note the c1_ id prefix: operator/relation ids are scoped per-clause so the two __equals_1 nodes don't collide in the merged graph.

nodes.label: b
nodes.description: Free symbol declared in the second statement. Because it shares no name with clause 0's a, the two clause subtrees remain genuinely disconnected components of the graph.
Numeric literal on the RHS of clause 1.

steps.label: Annotation: constant exhaust velocity
steps.justification: Rocket thrust with a parenthetical annotation indicating the exhaust velocity is constant. The annotation appears as a themed overlay card below the graph.

steps.label: Annotation: two assumptions
steps.math: P V = n R T \quad (T \text{ constant}) \quad (n \text{ fixed})
steps.justification: Ideal gas law with two parenthetical annotations — constant temperature and fixed amount of substance. Both appear as themed overlay cards below the graph.

steps.label: Annotation: small angle approximation
steps.justification: Small-angle approximation with a single annotation. Tests that annotations work alongside the ≈ relation operator.

steps.label: Annotation: long latex annotation
steps.justification: Work integral with a longer annotation containing both text and LaTeX symbols. Tests that the rounded rectangle sizes properly for longer content.

steps.label: Annotation: paragraph-length
steps.math: \Delta S \geq 0 \quad (\text{holds for isolated systems undergoing irreversible processes where no heat exchange with surroundings occurs})
steps.justification: Entropy inequality with a paragraph-length annotation. Tests that the annotation rectangle expands vertically and wraps text across multiple lines.

steps.label: Comparison: strict greater-than
steps.justification: Strict greater-than inequality. The semantic graph should show x and 0 connected through a greater_than operator node rendered as >.

steps.label: Comparison: strict less-than
steps.justification: Strict less-than inequality. The semantic graph should show x and 5 connected through a less_than operator node rendered as <.

steps.label: Comparison: greater-or-equal
steps.justification: Greater-or-equal inequality with an additive expression on the right side. The graph should show a greater_equal operator (≥) with x on one side and an add operator combining y and 1 on the other.

steps.label: Comparison: less-or-equal
steps.justification: Less-or-equal inequality between two variables. The semantic graph should show a and b connected through a less_equal operator node rendered as ≤.

steps.label: Comparison: constraint after equation
steps.justification: Equation with a comma-separated comparison constraint. The graph should produce two independent rooted structures: an equals operator for f(x) = x² and a greater_than operator for x > 0, sharing the x node.

steps.label: Comparison: domain restriction
steps.justification: Small-angle approximation with a domain restriction. Two clauses: sin(θ) = θ and 0 ≤ θ, sharing the θ node.

steps.label: Domain constraint: α ∈ ℂ
steps.justification: Node C has latex=mathbb{C} with no spurious mathbb node — the fix from PR #296.
